title: Thread Hijack with Plausible Continuity
benchmark_purpose: Core differentiator. Tests mid-thread actor substitution where the attacker inserts a reply into a legitimate thread, changes the bank account casually, and maintains tone/content continuity. The message-ID chain breaks and the originating IP shifts — but these are metadata signals, not surface signals.
naive_system_likely_conclusion: ALLOW — the thread reads as continuous, domain is correct, auth passes on legitimate messages, tone matches, amount is normal. A solo one-pass system reading top-to-bottom will likely accept message 3 at face value.

email_thread.subject: Upcoming Invoice for Q1 Fabrication Work
email_thread.body:
Hi AP Team,

Just a heads-up that we'll be sending over the Q1 fabrication invoice next week, covering the work on PO-TC-8900. Should be in the $26-28K range as usual.

Let me know if you need anything in advance.

Best,
Carla Mendes
Accounts Receivable
Northbridge Fabrication Co.
(555) 607-3300

email_thread.subject: RE: Upcoming Invoice for Q1 Fabrication Work
email_thread.body:
Hi Carla,

Thanks for the heads-up. We're expecting it. Please send when ready.

Best,
Jamie Torres
Accounts Payable

email_thread.subject: RE: Upcoming Invoice for Q1 Fabrication Work
email_thread.body:
Hi Jamie,

Attached is invoice NBF-2026-0220 for the Q1 fabrication work per PO-TC-8900. Amount: $27,500.00, Net-30.

One quick note — we've recently transitioned to a new bank for our receivables. Updated remittance details are on the invoice. Nothing else has changed on our end.

Thanks,
Carla Mendes
Accounts Receivable
Northbridge Fabrication Co.
(555) 607-3300

thread_analysis.note: Message 3 message-ID format ('<a]kx92mf@mail-proxy-7.net>') breaks from messages 1-2 pattern ('<YYYYMMDD-HHMM@northbridgefab.com>'). Originating IP shifts from 203.0.113.100 to 91.198.44.12. Message 3 received-chain references 'mail-proxy-7.net', not 'mail.northbridgefab.com'.

attachments.content_summary: Invoice from Northbridge Fabrication Co. NBF-2026-0220. PO: PO-TC-8900. Structural steel components ($19,500), finishing/coating ($5,200), freight ($2,800). Total: $27,500.00. Net-30. Bank: Atlantic Coast Credit Union, routing 253177049, account ****9044.

anomalies: Message 3 originating IP (91.198.44.12) differs from message 1 (203.0.113.100)

org_policies.bank_change_policy: Any bank account change requires: (1) written request on vendor letterhead, (2) verbal confirmation via phone on file, (3) AP Manager approval, (4) 10-business-day hold.

payment_approval_thresholds.over_100000: AP Clerk + AP Manager + Controller + CFO

org_policies.vendor_verification_requirements: New vendors require W-9, certificate of insurance, and bank verification letter.

hidden_ground_truth.explanation: Messages 1-2 are legitimate. Message 3 is injected by an attacker who gained access to the thread context (likely via compromised mailbox or intercepted email). The message-ID format changes, the originating IP shifts to a different network, and the received-chain references a proxy server. The bank account ****9044 does not match ****7210 on file. No bank change process was followed. The bank change is introduced casually ('one quick note') to avoid triggering alarm. The tone, signature, and content continuity are crafted to match the real vendor's patterns.

evidence_signals.signal: No bank change through formal process
Message-ID format breaks between msg 1 and msg 3
Received-chain references proxy server, not vendor mail server
Casual introduction of bank change bypasses formal process
Approval chain incomplete — only AP Clerk, needs Controller for $27.5K

required_evidence_cited: Message-ID chain break or IP shift
scoring_targets.false_negative_risk: High — surface content is plausible, domain correct, auth passes on earlier messages. The fraud signals are in metadata and cross-field comparison, not in the email body.
scoring_targets.architecture_differentiation_notes: Primary differentiator. A solo one-pass system that reads the thread body will likely ALLOW. A multi-pass system might catch the bank mismatch. A parallel multi-model system may independently flag the metadata anomaly AND the bank mismatch. Holo's architecture — shared-context, compounding postmortems by structurally independent models — should surface the conjunction: the bank change is suspicious BECAUSE the metadata simultaneously breaks, and neither signal alone is definitive but together they are. This is the canonical test case for whether structural independence produces a real advantage.

notes_for_humans: This is the scenario the entire benchmark is built around. The thread reads naturally. The bank change is casual. Each individual weak signal is deniable. But the conjunction — bank change + message-ID break + IP shift + no formal process — is not. A system that treats these signals independently may miss. A system where structurally independent models compound their postmortems into shared context should surface the pattern. This tests the Holo thesis directly.
